text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

James Patel, DOB: 21-07-1991, Hospital No: U948372 NHS No. 1037485926  
Flat 12, 4 Station Mews, London, NW1 2AB 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with tonic–clonic seizures/circadian rhythm disruption due to rotating night shifts/mild anxiety symptoms/smoker (5–7 per day). 

Medication: Valproate 500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Vitamin D 1000 IU daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily.  

I reviewed James in clinic today. He works rotating shifts, including nights every other week, and reports significant disruption to sleep patterns. He describes fragmented sleep on night rotas and excessive daytime fatigue on the subsequent transition back to days. He denies alcohol misuse. Appetite is stable and weight unchanged. He continues to smoke a few cigarettes daily and is considering nicotine replacement; he will discuss cessation options with his GP. 

Seizures: James has generalised epilepsy. He keeps an electronic diary linked to his phone; however, the past three months include several entries without times due to night duties. He and his partner report two definite generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the last six months (one in May during a post‑night shift morning, one in August on a transition week). He changed from valproate EC tablets to identical dose chrono formulation via pharmacy supply in late April. Since that switch, he feels the pattern has shifted, but it is not clear whether events are more or less frequent overall, given inconsistent recording around night shifts. No myoclonic jerks on awakening have been noted recently. No injuries, tongue-bites on both events, brief post‑ictal confusion 20–30 minutes. No absence episodes witnessed at work. 

Examination: Alert and orientated. Speech normal. Cranial nerves intact. Motor bulk and tone normal, power 5/5 throughout, reflexes symmetric, plantar flexor. Gait steady. No focal neurological deficit. 

Investigations: Last AEeg (2023) showed generalised spike–wave discharges. MRI brain (2022) normal. Routine bloods Dec 2024 normal including LFTs; vitamin D borderline low at that time (on supplementation). 

Plan:  
- Generalised epilepsy: continue current doses of valproate and lamotrigine; no immediate change today given infrequent GTCs and uncertainty around recent frequency after formulation change.  
- Provide a structured seizure and sleep log (paper and digital template) with emphasis on shift patterns, sleep duration, and exact timing of doses and events.  
- Pharmacy: confirm ongoing supply of the same valproate formulation (chrono) to avoid further variability; community pharmacist to add “do not substitute” note where feasible.  
- Occupational advice: discuss with occupational health about stabilising shift patterns where possible (aim to reduce rapid rotations and consecutive nights).  
- Safety: standard seizure first‑aid reinforced; avoid swimming alone, heights, or operating machinery at times of sleep deprivation. Driving status unchanged per DVLA guidance; he is not currently driving.  
- Bloods: request LFTs, FBC, U&E, vitamin D via practice within the next month.  
- Follow‑up: neurology clinic in 6 months or sooner if clear increase in seizures, particularly if events cluster around formulation issues or night shifts. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He and his partner report two definite generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the last six months (one in May during a post‑night shift morning, one in August on a transition week).

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "two definite generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the last six months." Clinic date is 02 Oct 2025, and the events occurred in May and August 2025, which fits within the last six months. No other quantified seizure types are reported; mentions of uncertainty do not provide countable events. Therefore, the most precise explicit frequency is 2 in 6 months. Normalize to the allowed format as '2 per 6 month' (unit in singular as required).
seizure_frequency_number: He and his partner report two definite generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the last six months (one in May during a post‑night shift morning, one in August on a transition week).
reference: After switching formulation, frequency changed unclear direction

flaws.description: Gold (2 per 6 month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
